title: L12 · Subjunctive (presente + imperfecto)
objective: The B1/B2 watershed: the subjunctive mood. Spanish uses it MUCH more than French — every time there's doubt, emotion, wish, judgement, or unknown future. Once you can produce it, you sound competent.
strategy: Three parts: (1) FORM the present subjunctive. (2) recognise the TRIGGERS — verbs and phrases that demand subjunctive in the next clause. (3) imperfect subjunctive for hypotheticals. Memory hook: WEIRDO — Wishes, Emotions, Impersonal expressions, Recommendations, Doubt/Denial, Ojalá. If the main clause expresses one of these, the next clause goes subjunctive.

sections.title: Present subjunctive — how to form it
sections.note: Simple rule: take the 'yo' form of the present indicative, drop the -o, add OPPOSITE-vowel endings. -ar verbs use 'e' endings; -er/-ir verbs use 'a' endings.

blocks.text: Step 1: yo form of present. Step 2: drop -o. Step 3: add the 'opposite' endings.
blocks.audioText: hablar → hablo → habl- → hable, hables, hable, hablemos, habléis, hablen
blocks.rule: AR endings flip to E (hable, hables, hable, hablemos, habléis, hablen). ER/IR endings flip to A (coma, comas, coma, comamos, comáis, coman / viva, vivas, viva, vivamos, viváis, vivan).

blocks.text: hablar — present subjunctive
blocks.audioText: hable, hables, hable

examples.translation: I want you to talk with me. / Je veux que tu parles avec moi.

blocks.text: comer — present subjunctive
blocks.audioText: coma, comas, coma

examples.translation: It's important that you eat vegetables. / Il est important que tu manges des légumes.

blocks.text: vivir — present subjunctive
blocks.audioText: viva, vivas, viva

blocks.text: If the 'yo' form is irregular, the subjunctive carries that irregularity through ALL forms
blocks.audioText: tener (tengo) → tenga, tengas, tenga; hacer (hago) → haga; salir (salgo) → salga; conocer (conozco) → conozca
blocks.rule: This is the magic trick: many 'irregular' subjunctives are completely regular IF you start from the yo form. tengo → teng- → tenga. hago → hag- → haga. conozco → conozc- → conozca.

blocks.text: 6 truly irregular subjunctives — memorise these
blocks.audioText: ser → sea; ir → vaya; haber → haya; saber → sepa; dar → dé; estar → esté
blocks.rule: These don't follow the yo-form trick. Their subjunctive stems are unique. ALL six are high-frequency.

examples.text: ser → sea, seas, sea, seamos, seáis, sean
examples.translation: (that) I be / sois — note 'sea' has no accent (different from 'sé' = 'I know')

examples.text: ir → vaya, vayas, vaya, vayamos, vayáis, vayan
examples.translation: (that) I go / aille

examples.text: haber → haya, hayas, haya, hayamos, hayáis, hayan
examples.translation: (that) there be / aie (auxiliary)

examples.text: saber → sepa, sepas, sepa, sepamos, sepáis, sepan
examples.translation: (that) I know / sache

examples.text: dar → dé, des, dé, demos, deis, den
examples.translation: (that) I give / donne — note accent on 'dé' (distinguishes from 'de'='of')

examples.text: estar → esté, estés, esté, estemos, estéis, estén
examples.translation: (that) I be (state) / sois

sections.title: WEIRDO — when to use the subjunctive
sections.note: Memorise the WEIRDO acronym: Wishes, Emotions, Impersonal expressions, Recommendations, Doubt/Denial, Ojalá. If the main clause does one of these AND has 'que', the next clause goes subjunctive.

blocks.text: W — Wishes / Wanting (querer que, esperar que, preferir que, desear que)
blocks.audioText: Quiero que vengas. Espero que estés bien. Prefiero que hables despacio.
blocks.rule: Whenever the wish is for SOMEONE ELSE to do something, you need 'que + subjunctive'. If you wish for YOURSELF, use infinitive (Quiero ir = I want to go).

examples.text: Quiero que vengas a mi fiesta.
examples.audioText: Quiero que vengas a mi fiesta.
examples.translation: I want you to come to my party. / Je veux que tu viennes à ma fête.
I hope you're well. / J'espère que tu vas bien.
examples.ipa: Spanish 'esperar que' takes subjunctive; French 'espérer que' takes indicative. Trap for French speakers!

examples.translation: I'd prefer you to speak more slowly.

blocks.text: E — Emotions (alegrarse de que, sentir que, temer que, estar contento de que)
blocks.audioText: Me alegro de que estés aquí. Siento que estés triste.
blocks.rule: Any emotional reaction to a fact triggers subjunctive. Even if the fact is true and known.

examples.text: Me alegro de que estés aquí.
examples.audioText: Me alegro de que estés aquí.
examples.translation: I'm glad (that) you're here. / Je suis content que tu sois là.
I'm sorry you're sick. / Je suis désolé que tu sois malade.

examples.text: Me sorprende que no lo sepas.
examples.audioText: Me sorprende que no lo sepas.
examples.translation: It surprises me that you don't know. / Cela me surprend que tu ne le saches pas.

blocks.text: I — Impersonal expressions (es necesario que, es importante que, es bueno que, es posible que)
blocks.audioText: Es necesario que estudies. Es importante que vengas.
blocks.rule: ANY 'es + adjective + que' expression triggers subjunctive. Exception: 'es verdad que' and 'es seguro que' (= certainty) take INDICATIVE.

examples.translation: It's important that you study. / Il est important que tu étudies.
It might rain tomorrow. / Il se peut qu'il pleuve demain.
You need to come. / Il faut que tu viennes.
It's true that he's coming. / Il est vrai qu'il vient.

blocks.text: R — Recommendations / Requests (recomendar que, sugerir que, pedir que, decir que, aconsejar que)
blocks.audioText: Te recomiendo que vayas. Le pido que me ayude.
blocks.rule: Asking, ordering, recommending — anything where you want someone to act — triggers subjunctive.

examples.text: Te recomiendo que veas esa película.
examples.audioText: Te recomiendo que veas esa película.
examples.translation: I recommend you watch that film. / Je te recommande de voir ce film.

examples.text: El médico me dice que coma menos.
examples.audioText: El médico me dice que coma menos.
examples.translation: The doctor tells me to eat less. / Le médecin me dit de manger moins. — note: 'decir que' = command takes subjunctive; 'decir que' = reporting fact takes indicative

blocks.text: D — Doubt / Denial (dudar que, no creer que, no pensar que, negar que)
blocks.audioText: Dudo que venga. No creo que sea verdad.
blocks.rule: Whenever you express DOUBT or NEGATION about a claim, the next clause goes subjunctive. The affirmative versions (creer que, pensar que) take INDICATIVE — that's the trick.

examples.translation: I doubt he'll come. / Je doute qu'il vienne.
I don't think it's true. / Je ne crois pas que ce soit vrai.
I believe it's true. / Je crois que c'est vrai.

blocks.text: O — Ojalá! (= 'I hope', 'if only')
blocks.audioText: Ojalá que venga. Ojalá llueva mañana.
blocks.rule: 'Ojalá' (from Arabic 'inshallah') is a fixed expression meaning 'I hope' or 'if only'. ALWAYS triggers subjunctive. The 'que' is optional. Different tenses of subjunctive = different time/likelihood: ojalá + present subj (hopefully X happens), ojalá + imperfect subj (if only X were happening / I wish).

examples.translation: I hope he comes tomorrow. / J'espère qu'il viendra demain.
If only it would rain. / Si seulement il pleuvait. — imperfect subj for unlikely wishes

sections.title: Subjunctive with 'cuando', 'hasta que', 'en cuanto' for FUTURE events
sections.note: Spanish marks future uncertainty with subjunctive after time conjunctions. Trips up French/English speakers — both languages use indicative here.

blocks.text: Past time → indicative. Future time → subjunctive.
blocks.audioText: Cuando llego a casa, ceno. (habit, indicative) — Cuando llegue a casa, cenaré. (future, subjunctive)
blocks.rule: When the 'cuando' refers to a HABIT or PAST, use indicative. When it refers to FUTURE (uncertain), use subjunctive.

examples.translation: When I get home, I have dinner. (every day) / Quand je rentre, je dîne.
When I get home (later today), I'll have dinner. / Quand je rentrerai, je dînerai.
When I got home, I had dinner. (past) / Quand je suis rentré, j'ai dîné. — pure indicative

blocks.text: hasta que / en cuanto / antes de que — same future-subjunctive rule
blocks.audioText: Esperaré hasta que vengas. En cuanto llegues, llámame.
blocks.rule: All time conjunctions that point to future trigger subjunctive on the future side. 'antes de que' (before) ALWAYS triggers subjunctive, even in past.

examples.translation: I'll wait until you come. / J'attendrai jusqu'à ce que tu viennes.
As soon as you arrive, call me. / Dès que tu arriveras, appelle-moi.

examples.text: Hablamos antes de que se vaya.
examples.audioText: Hablamos antes de que se vaya.
examples.translation: We talk before he leaves. / Nous parlons avant qu'il s'en aille. — antes de que ALWAYS subj

sections.title: Imperfect subjunctive — for hypotheticals and past triggers
sections.note: Used in 'if I were...' constructions and any subjunctive trigger in past context. Has two valid forms (-ra and -se) — both correct, -ra is more common in speech.

blocks.text: Take the 'ellos' form of preterite, drop -ron, add -ra / -ras / -ra / -´ramos / -rais / -ran
blocks.audioText: hablaron → habla- → hablara, hablaras, hablara, habláramos, hablarais, hablaran
blocks.rule: Stem = ellos preterite minus -ron. Endings: -ra / -ras / -ra / -ramos / -rais / -ran. Nosotros has a written accent: habláramos.

blocks.text: hablar — imperfect subjunctive
blocks.audioText: hablara, hablaras, hablara

blocks.text: tener — imperfect subjunctive (from tuvieron)
blocks.audioText: tuviera, tuvieras, tuviera

examples.translation: If I had money, I would travel. / Si j'avais de l'argent, je voyagerais.

blocks.text: ser/ir — imperfect subjunctive (from fueron)
blocks.audioText: fuera, fueras, fuera
blocks.translation: (that) I were / went — (que) je fusse, allasse

examples.text: Si yo fuera tú, lo haría.
examples.audioText: Si yo fuera tú, lo haría.
examples.translation: If I were you, I'd do it. / Si j'étais toi, je le ferais.

blocks.text: Hypothetical 'if' sentences: si + imperf. subj, + conditional
blocks.audioText: Si tuviera tiempo, iría. Si fuera rico, compraría una casa.
blocks.rule: The unreal/unlikely 'if' construction. Always: imperfect subjunctive in the 'si' clause, conditional in the result clause. Same pattern as French 'si + imparfait, + conditionnel'.

examples.text: Si tuviera tiempo, iría al cine.
examples.audioText: Si tuviera tiempo, iría al cine.
examples.translation: If I had time, I'd go to the cinema. / Si j'avais le temps, j'irais au cinéma.

examples.text: Si fuera rico, viajaría por el mundo.
examples.audioText: Si fuera rico, viajaría por el mundo.
examples.translation: If I were rich, I'd travel the world. / Si j'étais riche, je voyagerais à travers le monde.
If you could choose, what would you do? / Si tu pouvais choisir, que ferais-tu?

blocks.text: Past WEIRDO triggers also take imperfect subjunctive
blocks.audioText: Quería que vinieras. Esperaba que estuvieras bien.
blocks.rule: If the main clause is in past tense (quería, esperaba, era importante que...), the subordinate goes to IMPERFECT subjunctive. Tense agreement.

examples.text: Quería que vinieras a mi cumpleaños.
examples.audioText: Quería que vinieras a mi cumpleaños.
examples.translation: I wanted you to come to my birthday. / Je voulais que tu viennes à mon anniversaire.

examples.text: Era importante que llegáramos a tiempo.
examples.audioText: Era importante que llegáramos a tiempo.
examples.translation: It was important that we arrived on time. / Il était important que nous arrivions à l'heure.

sections.title: What comes after B2
sections.note: Quick map of what's left so you know where you're going.

blocks.text: Present perfect (he hablado) and pluperfect (había hablado)
blocks.audioText: He comido. Había estudiado.
blocks.rule: Compound tenses with 'haber' + participle. Used a lot in Spain for recent past ('he comido' = I've eaten). LatAm prefers the preterite. Easy once you know how it works in French (j'ai mangé).

blocks.text: Pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect for past hypotheticals
blocks.audioText: Si hubiera sabido, no habría venido.
blocks.rule: 'If I had known, I wouldn't have come'. Pattern: si + hubiera + participle, + habría + participle. Same structure as French (si j'avais su, je ne serais pas venu).

blocks.text: Commands (imperativo) — affirmative and negative
blocks.audioText: ¡Habla! ¡No hables! ¡Vengan!
blocks.rule: Negative commands use the subjunctive (no hables, no comas). Positive informal commands use the él form (habla, come). Positive nosotros/vosotros/usted have their own forms.

blocks.text: Connectors / Conectores discursivos for B2+
blocks.audioText: sin embargo, por lo tanto, además, mientras que, en cambio
blocks.rule: 'sin embargo' (however), 'por lo tanto' (therefore), 'además' (moreover), 'mientras que' (whereas), 'en cambio' (on the other hand). Native-sounding discourse glue.

blocks.text: Object pronoun stacking + reflexive verb mastery
blocks.audioText: Se me cayó. Se nos olvidaron las llaves.
blocks.rule: The 'se' impersonal + the 'se' accidental ('se me cayó' = it fell on me / I dropped it). This is the kind of native expressiveness that distinguishes B2 from B1.
